annotations.label: Mathematical logic

anchor.snippet: Mathematical logic is the study of formal logic within mathematics

annotations.note: A meta-level field description, not a mathematical statement that could be a Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Formalization of mathematics in set theory

anchor.snippet: almost all ordinary mathematics can be formalized in terms of sets

annotations.note: This is a philosophical/meta claim about formalizability rather than a provable statement.

annotations.label: Arithmetic as theory of natural numbers

anchor.snippet: the term arithmetic refers to the theory of the natural numbers

annotations.note: Terminological remark; ℕ exists in Mathlib but 'arithmetic = theory of naturals' is not a declaration.

annotations.label: Peano axioms

anchor.snippet: published a set of axioms for arithmetic that came to bear his name

annotations.note: ℕ realizes the Peano axioms but Mathlib has no bundled `PeanoAxioms` axiomatization object.

annotations.label: Induction characterization of the naturals (Dedekind)

anchor.snippet: the natural numbers are uniquely characterized by their induction properties

annotations.note: Mathlib uses the built-in induction principle but does not formalize Dedekind's categoricity/uniqueness characterization.

annotations.label: Uniqueness of the naturals up to isomorphism

anchor.snippet: including the uniqueness of the set of natural numbers

annotations.note: No theorem stating any two models of the Peano/Dedekind axioms are isomorphic was found.

annotations.label: Independence of the parallel postulate

anchor.snippet: the independence of the parallel postulate

annotations.note: Mathlib does not axiomatize synthetic geometry or prove independence of the parallel postulate.

annotations.label: Theorem that a line contains at least two points

anchor.snippet: the theorem that a line contains at least two points

annotations.note: No Hilbert-style incidence-geometry axiomatization with this derived theorem exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hilbert's axioms for geometry

anchor.snippet: developed a complete set of axioms for geometry

annotations.note: Mathlib treats geometry analytically (EuclideanSpace) and has no formalization of Hilbert's synthetic axioms.

annotations.label: Nowhere-differentiable continuous functions

anchor.snippet: such as nowhere-differentiable continuous functions

annotations.note: No construction of a Weierstrass-type nowhere-differentiable continuous function was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: (ε, δ)-definition of limit (Bolzano)

anchor.snippet: definition of limit and continuous functions was already developed by Bolzano

annotations.note: The ε–δ characterization of limits/continuity is `Metric.continuousAt_iff` (and `Metric.tendsto_nhds`).

annotations.label: Cauchy's definition of continuity

anchor.snippet: defined continuity in terms of infinitesimals

annotations.note: Mathlib has hyperreals, standard part, and `Infinitesimal`, but not a continuity-via-infinitesimals definition as the primary one.

annotations.label: Dedekind cuts definition of the reals

anchor.snippet: a definition of the real numbers in terms of Dedekind cuts of rational numbers

annotations.note: Mathlib constructs ℝ via Cauchy sequences rather than Dedekind cuts, though a general Dedekind–MacNeille completion exists.

annotations.label: Reals and naturals have different cardinalities

anchor.snippet: proved that the reals and the natural numbers have different cardinalities

annotations.note: `Cardinal.not_countable_real` (with `Cardinal.mk_real = 𝔠`) shows ℝ is not countable, hence differs in cardinality from ℕ.

annotations.label: Uncountability of the reals (diagonal argument)

anchor.snippet: a new proof of the uncountability of the real numbers that introduced the diagonal argument

annotations.note: Uncountability of ℝ is `Cardinal.not_countable_real`, though Mathlib's proof uses cardinal arithmetic rather than the literal diagonal argument.

annotations.label: Cantor's theorem

anchor.snippet: Cantor's theorem that no set can have the same cardinality as its powerset

annotations.note: `Function.cantor_surjective`/`cantor_injective` (and `Cardinal.cantor`) state no surjection from a type to its powerset exists.

annotations.label: Well-ordering theorem (Zermelo)

anchor.snippet: gave a proof that every set could be well-ordered

annotations.note: `exists_wellFoundedLT` (Zermelo's theorem) gives every type a well-order, generalizing 'every set can be well-ordered'.

annotations.label: Burali-Forti paradox

anchor.snippet: the Burali-Forti paradox shows that the collection of all ordinal numbers cannot form a set

annotations.note: `ZFSet.isOrdinal_notMem_univ` is explicitly the Burali-Forti paradox: the ordinals form a proper class.

annotations.label: Russell's paradox

anchor.snippet: Bertrand Russell discovered Russell's paradox in 1901

annotations.note: No dedicated formalization of Russell's paradox was found; the type-theoretic foundation precludes the naive comprehension it relies on.

annotations.label: Independence of choice with urelements (Fraenkel)

anchor.snippet: the axiom of choice cannot be proved from the axioms of Zermelo's set theory with urelements

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize permutation models or independence results over set theory with urelements.

annotations.label: Independence of the axiom of choice in ZF (Cohen)

anchor.snippet: the axiom of choice is unprovable in ZF

annotations.note: Forcing and the independence of AC from ZF are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Löwenheim–Skolem theorem

anchor.snippet: first-order logic cannot control the cardinalities of infinite structures

annotations.note: The downward Löwenheim–Skolem theorem is `exists_elementarySubstructure_card_eq`; the upward direction is not separately stated.

annotations.label: Skolem's paradox

anchor.snippet: any such formalization has a countable model

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize a first-order axiomatization of set theory or its countable model giving Skolem's paradox.

annotations.label: Gödel's completeness theorem

anchor.snippet: which establishes a correspondence between syntax and semantics in first-order logic

annotations.note: Mathlib's model theory has semantics but no proof/derivation system, so completeness (provable ⇔ valid) is not formalized.

annotations.label: Compactness theorem

anchor.snippet: demonstrating the finitary nature of first-order logical consequence

annotations.note: The compactness theorem is `Theory.isSatisfiable_iff_isFinitelySatisfiable`.

annotations.label: Gödel's incompleteness theorem

anchor.snippet: the incompleteness (in a different meaning of the word) of all sufficiently strong, effective first-order theories

annotations.note: Gödel's incompleteness theorems are not formalized in Mathlib (no internal provability predicate / arithmetization).

annotations.label: Unprovability of consistency in-system

anchor.snippet: a consistency proof of any sufficiently strong, effective axiom system cannot be obtained in the system itself

annotations.note: The second incompleteness theorem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gentzen's consistency proof of arithmetic

anchor.snippet: Gentzen proved the consistency of arithmetic using a finitistic system together with a principle of transfinite induction

annotations.note: Gentzen's ordinal-analysis consistency proof of PA is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gödel's Dialectica consistency proof

anchor.snippet: reduces the consistency of classical arithmetic to that of intuitionistic arithmetic in higher types

annotations.note: The Dialectica interpretation is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: First-order logic

anchor.snippet: First-order logic is a particular formal system of logic

annotations.note: `FirstOrder.Language` with terms/formulas (Syntax) and structures/satisfaction (Semantics) formalizes first-order logic.

annotations.label: Löwenheim–Skolem theorem (precise)

anchor.snippet: if a set of sentences in a countable first-order language has an infinite model

annotations.note: The downward Löwenheim–Skolem theorem is formalized as `exists_elementarySubstructure_card_eq`.

annotations.label: Gödel's completeness theorem (precise)

anchor.snippet: established the equivalence between semantic and syntactic definitions of logical consequence in first-order logic

annotations.note: No deductive system or completeness theorem exists in Mathlib's first-order logic.

annotations.label: Compactness theorem (precise)

anchor.snippet: a set of sentences has a model if and only if every finite subset has a model

annotations.note: `Theory.isSatisfiable_iff_isFinitelySatisfiable` is exactly: a theory is satisfiable iff every finite subset is.

annotations.label: First incompleteness theorem

anchor.snippet: for any consistent, effectively given (defined below) logical system that is capable of interpreting arithmetic

annotations.note: Gödel's first incompleteness theorem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gödel sentence for Peano axioms

anchor.snippet: the Gödel sentence holds for the natural numbers but cannot be proved

annotations.note: Construction of a Gödel sentence for PA is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Effectively given logical system

anchor.snippet: a logical system is said to be effectively given if it is possible to decide

annotations.note: Mathlib has computability but no definition of an 'effectively given' (recursively axiomatized) logical system.

annotations.label: Non-elementarily-equivalent models from incompleteness

anchor.snippet: any sufficiently strong, consistent, effective first-order theory has models that are not elementarily equivalent

annotations.note: This corollary of incompleteness is not formalized, as incompleteness itself is absent.

annotations.label: Second incompleteness theorem

anchor.snippet: no sufficiently strong, consistent, effective axiom system for arithmetic can prove its own consistency

annotations.note: The second incompleteness theorem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Infinitary logics

anchor.snippet: which allow for formulas to provide an infinite amount of information

annotations.note: Mathlib's logic supports only finitary first-order formulas; infinitary logics are not formalized.

annotations.label: The infinitary logic L_ω1ω

anchor.snippet: In this logic, quantifiers may only be nested to finite depths

annotations.note: The logic L_ω1ω is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Higher-order logics

anchor.snippet: Higher-order logics allow for quantification not only of elements of the domain of discourse

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize higher-order logic as an object of study (the ambient type theory is not such a formalization).

annotations.label: Higher-order logic lacks completeness/compactness

anchor.snippet: they do not satisfy analogues of the completeness and compactness theorems from first-order logic

annotations.note: This metatheoretic property of higher-order logic is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Fixed-point logics

anchor.snippet: that allow inductive definitions

annotations.note: Fixed-point logics are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Extension of first-order logic

anchor.snippet: One can formally define an extension of first-order logic

annotations.note: Mathlib has no abstract framework of 'logics' in which one defines extensions of first-order logic.

annotations.label: Lindström's theorem

anchor.snippet: the only extension of first-order logic satisfying both the compactness theorem and the downward Löwenheim–Skolem theorem is first-order logic

annotations.note: Lindström's theorem (abstract model theory) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Modal logics

anchor.snippet: Modal logics include additional modal operators

annotations.note: Modal logics are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Intuitionistic logic and excluded middle

anchor.snippet: Intuitionistic logic specifically does not include the law of the excluded middle

annotations.note: Mathlib works in classical logic (`Classical.em`) but does not formalize intuitionistic logic as a system.

annotations.label: Provably total intuitionistic functions are computable

anchor.snippet: any provably total function in intuitionistic arithmetic is computable

annotations.note: This metatheorem about Heyting arithmetic is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Algebraic logic

anchor.snippet: Algebraic logic uses the methods of abstract algebra to study the semantics of formal logics

annotations.note: Algebraic logic as a discipline is not formalized, though algebraic structures like Boolean algebras exist.

annotations.label: Boolean algebras for propositional truth values

anchor.snippet: A fundamental example is the use of Boolean algebras to represent truth values in classical propositional logic

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `BooleanAlgebra` and the instance `Prop.instBooleanAlgebra`, but not a propositional-logic semantics interpreted into Boolean algebras.

annotations.label: Set theory

anchor.snippet: Set theory is the study of sets

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes a model of set theory via `ZFSet`, but the discipline-level definition is meta.

annotations.label: Zermelo–Fraenkel set theory (ZF)

anchor.snippet: was extended slightly to become Zermelo–Fraenkel set theory (ZF)

annotations.note: `ZFSet` is the von Neumann cumulative hierarchy realizing the ZFC axioms (proved as theorems).

annotations.label: Alternative set theories (NBG, MK, NF)

anchor.snippet: Other formalizations of set theory have been proposed, including von Neumann–Bernays–Gödel set theory (NBG)

annotations.note: Mathlib has `Class` (proper classes, NBG-flavored) but does not axiomatize NBG, MK, or NF as distinct theories.

annotations.label: Axiom of choice

anchor.snippet: given a collection of nonempty sets there is a single set C that contains exactly one element from each set in the collection

annotations.note: `Classical.axiomOfChoice` states a family of nonempty types admits a choice function (and `Classical.choice` is the underlying axiom).

annotations.label: Banach–Tarski paradox

anchor.snippet: the axiom of choice can be used to decompose a solid ball into a finite number of pieces

annotations.note: The Banach–Tarski paradox is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Consistency of CH with ZF (Gödel)

anchor.snippet: the continuum hypothesis cannot be disproven from the axioms of Zermelo–Fraenkel set theory

annotations.note: Gödel's constructible-universe consistency of CH is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Independence of CH (Cohen)

anchor.snippet: the continuum hypothesis cannot be proven from the axioms of Zermelo–Fraenkel set theory

annotations.note: Cohen forcing and the independence of CH are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Large cardinals

anchor.snippet: Large cardinals are cardinal numbers with particular properties so strong that the existence of such cardinals cannot be proved in ZFC

annotations.note: Mathlib defines a specific large cardinal notion, `Cardinal.IsInaccessible`, but not the general large-cardinal framework.

annotations.label: Inaccessible cardinal implies Con(ZFC)

anchor.snippet: already implies the consistency of ZFC

annotations.note: There is no formalized Con(ZFC) statement, so this consistency-strength result is absent.

annotations.label: Determinacy

anchor.snippet: Determinacy refers to the possible existence of winning strategies for certain two-player games

annotations.note: Infinite-game determinacy (e.g. AD, Borel determinacy) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Model theory

anchor.snippet: Model theory studies the models of various formal theories

annotations.note: Mathlib has a substantial first-order model theory framework (languages, theories, `ModelType`), though the field's definition is meta.

annotations.label: Theory and model

anchor.snippet: a theory is a set of formulas in a particular formal logic and signature

annotations.note: `FirstOrder.Language.Theory` is a set of sentences and `M ⊨ T` / `ModelType` formalize models.

annotations.label: Elementary class

anchor.snippet: The set of all models of a particular theory is called an elementary class

annotations.note: The collection of models of a theory is captured by `Theory.ModelType`/`ModelsTheory`, but 'elementary class' is not a named definition.

annotations.label: Quantifier elimination for real-closed fields (Tarski)

anchor.snippet: Tarski established quantifier elimination for real-closed fields

annotations.note: Real-closed fields are defined (`IsRealClosed`) but quantifier elimination for them is not formalized.

annotations.label: Morley's categoricity theorem

anchor.snippet: if a first-order theory in a countable language is categorical in some uncountable cardinality

annotations.note: `Categorical` and the Łoś–Vaught test (`Categorical.isComplete`) exist, but Morley's uncountable-categoricity transfer theorem is not proved.

annotations.label: Countable-model consequence of CH

anchor.snippet: A trivial consequence of the continuum hypothesis is that a complete theory with less than continuum many nonisomorphic countable models can have only countably many

annotations.note: This counting-of-countable-models consequence of CH is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Vaught's conjecture

anchor.snippet: says that this is true even independently of the continuum hypothesis

annotations.note: Vaught's conjecture is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Recursion theory

anchor.snippet: studies the properties of computable functions and the Turing degrees

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes computable/partial-recursive functions and Turing degrees (`Computability.TuringDegree`), covering the objects of recursion theory.

annotations.label: Robustness of the computable functions

anchor.snippet: The fundamental results establish a robust, canonical class of computable functions with numerous independent, equivalent characterizations using Turing machines

annotations.note: Mathlib proves equivalence between Turing-machine computability and partial recursive functions, but not the full slate of equivalent models.

annotations.label: Generalized recursion theory

anchor.snippet: Generalized recursion theory extends the ideas of recursion theory to computations that are no longer necessarily finite

annotations.note: Higher/generalized recursion theory (e.g. hyperarithmetic, α-recursion) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Algorithmically unsolvable problem

anchor.snippet: a decision problem or function problem is algorithmically unsolvable if there is no possible computable algorithm that returns the correct answer for all legal inputs to the problem

annotations.note: Unsolvability is expressed as `¬ ComputablePred`; the predicate `ComputablePred` is defined but 'unsolvable problem' is not a standalone definition.

annotations.label: Undecidability of the Entscheidungsproblem

anchor.snippet: showed that the Entscheidungsproblem is algorithmically unsolvable

annotations.note: Undecidability of first-order validity is not formalized (Mathlib has no proof system / decidability statement for FOL).

annotations.label: Unsolvability of the halting problem

anchor.snippet: establishing the unsolvability of the halting problem

annotations.note: `ComputablePred.halting_problem` states the halting predicate is not computable.

annotations.label: Unsolvability of the word problem for groups

anchor.snippet: The word problem for groups was proved algorithmically unsolvable by Pyotr Novikov

annotations.note: The Novikov–Boone unsolvability of the group word problem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Busy beaver problem

anchor.snippet: The busy beaver problem, developed by Tibor Radó in 1962, is another well-known example

annotations.note: The busy beaver function and its noncomputability are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hilbert's tenth problem

anchor.snippet: Hilbert's tenth problem asked for an algorithm to determine whether a multivariate polynomial equation with integer coefficients has a solution in the integers

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes Diophantine sets (`Dioph`), the central object of Hilbert's tenth problem, but not the decision-problem statement itself.

annotations.label: Unsolvability of Hilbert's tenth problem (Matiyasevich)

anchor.snippet: The algorithmic unsolvability of the problem was proved by Yuri Matiyasevich in 1970

annotations.note: `pow_dioph` (the power function is Diophantine) is a key step of the MRDP theorem, but the full RE=Diophantine result and resulting unsolvability are not formalized.

annotations.label: Proof theory

anchor.snippet: Proof theory is the study of formal proofs in various logical deduction systems

annotations.note: Mathlib has no formalized deduction systems or formal proofs as objects, so proof theory is absent.

annotations.label: Predicative development of real analysis (Weyl)

anchor.snippet: who showed it is possible to develop a large part of real analysis using only predicative methods

annotations.note: Predicative reverse-mathematics development of analysis is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gödel–Gentzen negative translation

anchor.snippet: it is possible to embed (or translate ) classical logic into intuitionistic logic

annotations.note: The double-negation/negative translation embedding classical into intuitionistic logic is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Curry–Howard correspondence

anchor.snippet: The Curry–Howard correspondence between proofs and programs relates to proof theory

annotations.note: Curry–Howard underlies Lean's type theory but is not stated as a formalized theorem in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Fagin's theorem

anchor.snippet: NP is precisely the set of languages expressible by sentences of existential second-order logic

annotations.note: Fagin's descriptive-complexity theorem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Consistency of non-Euclidean geometry via spherical model

anchor.snippet: non-Euclidean geometry can be proved consistent by defining point to mean a point on a fixed sphere

annotations.note: No relative-consistency model of non-Euclidean geometry on a sphere is formalized in Mathlib.
